summary: API design is strong for a terminal application. The Lisp primitive bridge provides a clean, consistent functional interface. Core domain is well-modularized with clear separation of concerns. However, no REST/HTTP endpoints exist (TUI only), so traditional API assessment criteria (status codes, error shapes, versioning) do not apply. Bridge primitives follow naming conventions but lack formal documentation.

inconsistencies: Lisp bridge returns RuntimeError with string messages; core domain returns enum-based AlfredError
Error details (file paths, line numbers) not consistently structured across boundary

recommendations.title: Formalize Lisp bridge API contract with OpenAPI equivalent
recommendations.description: Bridge primitives are well-designed but lack formal specification. Create a machine-readable schema (e.g., JSON) documenting all primitives: parameter types, return types, error cases. Generate plugin documentation from schema.

recommendations.title: Standardize error return format across bridge/core boundary
recommendations.description: Lisp bridge uses RuntimeError { msg }, core uses Result<T, AlfredError>. Create a uniform error response format in Lisp that includes error code, message, and context. Map AlfredError variants to numeric codes.

recommendations.title: Add type metadata introspection for Lisp runtime
recommendations.description: Plugins can call primitives incorrectly (wrong arg count, types). Add (introspect-primitive name) that returns arity, parameter types, return type. Help plugins validate calls at load time.

recommendations.title: Document plugin API stability guarantees
recommendations.description: No versioning strategy exists. Document: which primitives are stable (never change), which are experimental (may change), deprecation policy. Enables plugin authors to set version constraints.
